title: ACK timing before long-running agent work
summary: Send a short user-facing acknowledgement before starting slow work, tool use, network calls, or delegation dispatch so the user knows the request was received before the long-running operation begins.
body_markdown:
# ACK timing before long-running agent work

## When to Use
Use this guidance when an agent is about to start work that may take more than a few seconds or depends on external execution, including:

- Tool or plugin calls.
- Network requests or API calls.
- Delegation to another agent, runner, or dispatch system.
- Long-running analysis, diagnostics, or debugging.
- Any workflow where the user may otherwise see no immediate response while the system begins work.

## Symptoms or Context
This pitfall appears when an agent receives a request and immediately begins slow or externally dispatched work without first responding to the user.

Common symptoms include:

- The user experiences the agent as silent, stalled, or unresponsive.
- A task is actually underway, but the user has no confirmation that the request was received.
- Tooling, network, or dispatch latency creates a visible gap before the first user-facing message.
- Users may retry, escalate, or assume the agent failed because no acknowledgement was emitted.

## Operating Rule
Before starting slow work, send a short acknowledgement first. Then proceed with the long-running operation.

The acknowledgement should be brief and should not claim the work is complete. It should only confirm receipt or indicate that processing is starting.

Examples:

- "Received — I’ll check that now."
- "On it."
- "I’m going to look into this."
- "Got it — starting the tool-based check now."

Avoid silence while beginning slow work, network calls, tool calls, or delegation dispatch.

## Procedure
1. Receive the user request.
2. Determine whether the next step is slow, external, delegated, or tool-based.
3. If yes, emit a short acknowledgement before starting that work.
4. Start the tool, network, dispatch, or long-running operation.
5. Return the final result when complete, including any useful status or failure information.

## Verify
Verify the behavior by checking that:

- The acknowledgement is emitted before the long-running step begins.
- Interaction logs show the ACK before tool calls, network calls, or delegation dispatch.
- The ACK does not falsely imply success or completion.
- The final response reports the actual outcome after the slow operation completes.

## Reusable Lessons
- Slow tool, network, dispatch, or long-running work can leave users without timely feedback.
- Send a short acknowledgement first, then proceed with the slow operation.
- Do not stay silent while beginning slow or externally dispatched work.
- An acknowledgement is not a success report; it only confirms that work is starting.

## Agent Use
Use this page when:

- You are about to use tools, network calls, dispatch, subagents, or any slow workflow.
- The user could otherwise wait without knowing whether the request was received.
- You are designing or reviewing an agent interaction pattern involving delayed execution.

Apply it by:

- Sending a short acknowledgement before starting the slow step.
- Keeping the ACK neutral: confirm receipt or start of work, not success.
- Continuing with the requested operation after the ACK.
- Reporting the actual result, error, or limitation after the operation completes.

Verify by:

- Confirming the ACK appears before the first slow/tool/network/dispatch action.
- Checking that the ACK is visible to the user, not only recorded internally.
- Ensuring the final answer is separate from the ACK and reflects the real outcome.

Do not use it when:

- The request can be answered immediately without tools, waiting, or delegation.
- A platform or protocol forbids intermediate user-facing messages.
- The ACK would be misleading because the agent has not actually accepted or cannot perform the task; in that case, respond directly with the limitation.

## Related Wiki Pages
- [[agents-can-mislead-users-if-failures-are-reported-as-successful-outcomes|Agents can mislead users if failures are reported as successful outcomes.]]
- [[openclaw-dispatch-routing-failures|OpenClaw dispatch routing failures]]
- [[openclaw-slack-replay-and-post-deploy-stability-failures|OpenClaw Slack replay and post-deploy stability failures]]

provenance.excerpt: Slow tool, network, dispatch, or long-running work can leave users without timely feedback. Send a short acknowledgement first, then proceed with the slow operation.

risk_notes: The core rule is strongly supported by the supplied evidence, but exact timing thresholds such as a specific number of seconds are operational guidance rather than directly evidenced.
Acknowledgements must not be worded as completion or success messages; final outcome reporting still needs to happen after the slow work completes.
